title: Pareto Optimal Coalitions of Fixed Size
journal: Journal of Mechanism and Institution Design
abstract: We tackle the problem of partitioning players into groups of fixed size, such as allocating eligible students to shared dormitory rooms. Each student submits preferences over the other individual students. We study several settings, which differ in the size of the rooms to be filled, the orderedness or completeness of the preferences, and the way of calculating the value of a coalition-based on the best or worst roommate in the coalition. In all cases, we determine the complexity of deciding the existence, and then finding a Pareto optimal assignment, and the complexity of verifying Pareto optimality for a given assignment.
summary_en: How do you partition students into shared rooms, optimally? This paper formalises the problem: players have preferences over others, rooms have fixed capacity, and you need to find a Pareto-optimal assignment. The authors characterise the complexity of deciding the existence of, finding, and verifying Pareto-optimal coalitions across multiple settings (different room sizes, different preference formats). A methodological contribution to assignment-problem complexity.
policy_relevance: Relevant to practical assignment-mechanism design in Hungarian institutional settings (university dormitories, refugee housing, shared-facility allocations). The paper informs which algorithms are feasible and which aren't. Caveat: worst-case complexity; real instances vary.
